request.question: The winner of the first season of "Next Great Baker" would win an apprenticeship at the bakery owned by who?

sentences: The Minnesota Vikings season was the franchise's 22nd season in the National Football League.
This was Minnesota's first season in the newly constructed Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome.
The team was looking to improve on its 7-9 record from 1981.
However, a players strike cancelled 7 of the team's 16 games, and each NFL team was only allowed to play 9 games.

The Vikings would win their opener against the Tampa Bay Buccaneers before losing the next week to the Buffalo Bills, a game in which they had a 19-0 lead before the Bills pulled off a miraculous comeback to win 23-22.
After the strike ended, the Vikings would get embarrassed in Green Bay against the Packers 26-7 before beating the Bears the next week 35-7 to sit at 2-2.
After a loss to the Dolphins, the Vikings would win their next 2 games to sit at 4-3.

In their final game of the season, they would upset the Dallas Cowboys 31-27 to clinch the NFC's 4th place spot in the playoffs (as divisions were ignored in 1982 and the standings were determined by conference).
In the playoffs, the Vikings would defeat the Atlanta Falcons 30-24 to reach the divisional round.
However, in that game, they would lose 21-7 to the eventual champion Redskins.

Next Great Baker: Latin America (also known as El desafío de Buddy: Latinoamérica) is an Latin American television series that airs on Discovery Home & Health: Latin America, hosted by Buddy Valastro, the star of his own reality series, "Cake Boss".
The show features contestants participating in challenges that test their baking and decorating skills.
Each week, a contestant is eliminated; the last contestant standing will win a grand prize package that varies by season.
Other prizes for winning a challenge or the week's competition are also offered during the series.
Carlo's Bake Shop, commonly known as Carlo's Bakery and also known as Carlo's City Hall Bake Shop, is a bakery in Hoboken, New Jersey, owned by Buddy Valastro.
The bakery has gained public attention as the setting of the TLC television series, "Cake Boss".
The second season of "Next Great Baker" was televised from November 28, 2011 to January 30, 2012 on TLC.
This season was set at the new Carlo's Bake Shop facility at Lackawanna Center in Jersey City, New Jersey.
"Next Great Baker" (also known as "Cake Boss: Next Great Baker") is an American reality television series that airs on TLC, hosted by Buddy Valastro, the star of his own reality series, "Cake Boss".
Season 3 commenced airing on November 26, 2012.

Batalha dos Confeiteiros (English: "Battle of the Bakers"), also known as Batalha dos Confeiteiros Brasil, is a Brazilian television series based on "Next Great Baker", hosted by Buddy Valastro, the star of his own reality series, "Cake Boss".
The series premiered Wednesday, September 30, 2015 at 10:30 p.m. on Record.
The third season of "Next Great Baker" aired from November 26, 2012 to February 11, 2013.
Like the previous season, this season was set at the Carlo's Bake Shop facility at Lackawanna Center in Jersey City, New Jersey.
Unlike the previous two seasons, the finale for this season took place outside of the greater New York City area – in this case, in Las Vegas, Nevada at The Venetian Las Vegas.
The first season of "Next Great Baker" was televised from December 6, 2010 to January 24, 2011 on TLC.
During this season, the last contestant standing would win $50,000 cash, a Chevrolet Cruze and an apprenticeship at Buddy's bakery, Carlo's Bake Shop in Hoboken, New Jersey.
Next Great Baker (also known as Cake Boss: Next Great Baker) is an American television series that airs on TLC, hosted by Buddy Valastro, the star of his own reality series, "Cake Boss".
The show features contestants participating in challenges that test their edible art baking and decorating skills.
Each week, a contestant will be eliminated; the last contestant standing will win a grand prize package that varies by season.
Other prizes for winning a challenge or the week's competition are also offered during the series.
The fourth season of "Next Great Baker" premiered on June 24, 2014.
Unlike other previous seasons, the teams competed in teams of two.

Other changes from the previous seasons include the addition of Jacques Torres and Bobbie Lloyd as judges, the addition of the final showdown, and the opportunity to work at Buddy's Las Vegas cake shop.
However, some aspects of the show were similar, including having ten competitors (or teams) as in season 1, and the $100,000 prize awarded in each of the previous seasons remained.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

The show features contestants participating in challenges that test their edible art baking and decorating skills. Next Great Baker: Latin America | Next Great Baker: Latin America (also known as El desafío de Buddy: Latinoamérica) is an Latin American television series that airs on Discovery Home & Health: Latin America, hosted by Buddy Valastro, the star of his own reality series, "Cake Boss".  The show features contestants participating in challenges that test their baking and decorating skills. Next Great Baker (season 2) | The second season of "Next Great Baker" was televised from November 28, 2011 to January 30, 2012 on TLC.  This season was set at the new Carlo's Bake Shop facility at Lackawanna Center in Jersey City, New Jersey.

2003 Cincinnati Bengals season | The 2003 Cincinnati Bengals season was the team's 36th year in professional football and its 34th with the National Football League.  The Bengals are now under first year Head Coach Marvin Lewis, who replaced Dick LeBeau who was fired following the 2002 season after the worst season in Bengals history.  The Bengals had the first pick overall in the 2003 NFL Draft and drafted Heisman Trophy winner Carson Palmer with the first pick.  After a slow start, the Bengals got hot winning at midseason, winning four straight games to stand at 7–5, entering a key Week 14 matchup with the Baltimore Ravens with a chance to win the division.  However in the key showdown for first place the Bengals showed they were not quite ready for primetime as they were beaten 31–13.  The Bengals would rebound to win their next game against the San Francisco 49ers, but at 8–6 the Bengals could not get that ninth win, losing their last two games to spoil an effort to earn their first winning season in 13 years, finishing at 8–8.

1969 Miami Dolphins season | The 1969 Miami Dolphins season was the team's fourth season, and their final season in the American Football League (AFL).  This would also be the final season for George Wilson as head coach, as Don Shula would be hired next season to start a dynasty for Miami and to coach the team for the next 25 seasons until 1995.  The team looked to improve on their 5-8-1 record from 1968.  However, the Dolphins would struggle from the seasons start, losing their first 3 games before tying the Oakland Raiders and losing their next 2 games to start the season 0-5-1.  After their week 7 win over the Buffalo Bills, the Dolphins would end the season with a 3-10-1 record.  It should also be noted that their week 10 loss to the Buffalo Bills would be the last time the Dolphins would lose to the Bills until 1980, as the Dolphins would win 20 straight against the Bills from 1970-1979.  This became known as "The Streak", as it set an NFL record for longest winning streak for one team against one opponent.

- Missing/ambiguous facts: none — passages unambiguously connect the apprenticeship to Buddy (Buddy Valastro).
- Next-hop retrieval: none required for answering; if verification desired, fetch the "Carlo's Bake Shop" page or the "Buddy Valastro" page and check owner/ownership statement.
- Conclusion: The bakery is owned by Buddy Valastro (supported by [1] and [2]).
hop2_docs: Carlo's Bake Shop | Carlo's Bake Shop, commonly known as Carlo's Bakery and also known as Carlo's City Hall Bake Shop, is a bakery in Hoboken, New Jersey, owned by Buddy Valastro.  The bakery has gained public attention as the setting of the TLC television series, "Cake Boss".

List of Cake Boss episodes | "Cake Boss" is an American reality television series, airing on the cable television network TLC.  The series is set at Carlo's Bake Shop in Hoboken, New Jersey and stars Buddy Valastro, the shop's owner, his retired mother, four older sisters, and two brothers-in-law in their everyday life of running a bakery store.

João Carlos Paes Mendonça | João Carlos Paes Mendonça (born 1938) is a Brazilian businessman.  Paes Mendonça was the owner of Grupo Bompreço and is the owner of Sistema Jornal do Commercio de Comunicação, plus having control of many shopping centers in Brazil.

Next Great Baker (season 2) | The second season of "Next Great Baker" was televised from November 28, 2011 to January 30, 2012 on TLC.  This season was set at the new Carlo's Bake Shop facility at Lackawanna Center in Jersey City, New Jersey.
Dozen Bake Shop | Dozen Bake Shop was a bakery in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.  While a variety of baked goods and other dishes were offered, cupcakes were Dozen's specialty and featured product.

Bakery | A bakery (a.k.a. baker's shop or bake shop) is an establishment that produces and sells flour-based food baked in an oven such as bread, cookies, cakes, pastries, and pies.  Some retail bakeries are also cafés, serving coffee and tea to customers who wish to consume the baked goods on the premises.

During this season, the last contestant standing would win $50,000 cash, a Chevrolet Cruze and an apprenticeship at Buddy's bakery, Carlo's Bake Shop in Hoboken, New Jersey.
